frameworks.name: accumulation-to-bound model with history-dependent initial state
frameworks.one_line: evidence accumulator whose start point is updated trial-by-trial under nonstationarity beliefs

frameworks.name: normative inference under nonstationary world beliefs
frameworks.one_line: optimal computation if the agent (mistakenly) treats the world as changing across trials

frameworks.name: lapse-rate models
frameworks.one_line: separate stochastic lapse parameter treated as distinct from history bias

core_question: Can trial-history biases and apparent lapses in perceptual decisions arise from a single cognitive process rather than two distinct ones?
key_assumptions: agent holds a mistaken belief that the world is nonstationary
history updates act on the initial state of the evidence accumulator
behaviour in fixed-stimulus tasks is well described by accumulation-to-bound
manipulations: task type: fixed-duration vs novel reaction-time perceptual decision task
two distinct rat decision-making datasets compared
key_findings: history biases and apparent lapses covary in prevalence across rats
a single model with history-dependent accumulator initial state captures both
model predictions hold across two independent rat datasets including a new RT task
improved within- and across-trial prediction of decision dynamics
interpretations: lapses and history biases are not distinct suboptimalities but reflect optimal inference under nonstationarity beliefs
history-dependent initial-state updates provide a mechanism for quasi-stochastic choice
